concise:
Ayah 1–11 · linked video: 01. Al-Hijr (Ayah 1-11)

Surah Al-Hijr: Ayahs 1-11

Ayah 1

الٓر ۚ تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَقُرْءَانٍ مُّبِينٍ

These are the miraculous signs of the Book and a clear, clarifying recitation.

This opening ayah establishes a profound duality about the nature of the Quran. Allah ﷻ describes it as two things simultaneously: الْكِتَـٰبِ - the recorded revelation preserved in the heavens in Al-Lawh al-Mahfudh, and قُرْءَانٍ مُّبِينٍ - a clear recitation being delivered here on earth through the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ.

By presenting both aspects together, Allah ﷻ creates an undeniable parallel between the original divine source and what is being recited. There exists no discrepancy between what is preserved above and what is being conveyed below. The means of delivery is secure, and this point becomes especially significant as we progress through the surah, because this security will be challenged by the disbelievers.

The word مُّبِينٍ carries both meanings: it is clear in itself, and it clarifies and makes things evident to those who receive it. This Quran doesn’t obscure truth - it illuminates it.

Ayah 2

رُّبَمَا يَوَدُّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَوْ كَانُوا۟ مُسْلِمِينَ

Perhaps the day is approaching when the disbelievers will wish that they had been Muslims.

This ayah serves as a powerful warning wrapped in understatement. The word رُّبَمَا - “perhaps” or “maybe” - creates an almost gentle tone, yet the meaning is absolute. There will come a day when those who reject faith will be consumed with regret, desperately wishing they had submitted to Allah ﷻ.

The disbelievers are going to wish they had been among the Muslims - not just believers, but specifically مُسْلِمِينَ, those who submitted. This establishes the foundation for what follows: until that day of regret arrives, the Prophet ﷺ is instructed to simply leave them to their choices.

Ayah 3

ذَرْهُمْ يَأْكُلُوا۟ وَيَتَمَتَّعُوا۟ وَيُلْهِهِمُ ٱلْأَمَلُ ۖ فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ

Leave them to eat and enjoy themselves, and let hope distract them, for they will soon come to know.

This is a command to the Prophet ﷺ that might seem surprising at first. ذَرْهُمْ - leave them alone. Let them continue in their state. يَأْكُلُوا۟ وَيَتَمَتَّعُوا۟ - let them eat and take pleasure in this worldly life.

The most striking element here is وَيُلْهِهِمُ ٱلْأَمَلُ- and let hope distract them. The word أَمَلُ refers to prolonged hopes and long-term aspirations. Most people live for these extended plans: first I’ll open a store, then expand the business, then buy this house, then accomplish that goal. These hopes, typically centered around wealth and material security, become all-consuming.

Allah ﷻ describes these hopes as something that يُلْهِهِمُ- distracts and preoccupies them completely. The subject doing the distracting is their own hopes and aspirations. They become so absorbed in their worldly plans that they lose sight of what truly matters.

The ayah concludes with فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ- very soon they will come to know. This isn’t a distant, theoretical warning. It’s imminent. They will discover the reality of what they denied.

Ayah 4

وَمَآ أَهْلَكْنَا مِن قَرْيَةٍ إِلَّا وَلَهَا كِتَابٌ مَّعْلُومٌ

We have never destroyed any town except that it had a known decree.

This ayah introduces a crucial principle about divine justice: Allah ﷻ does not destroy arbitrarily. Every nation that faced destruction had كِتَابٌ مَّعْلُومٌ- a known book, meaning a known, declared, and predetermined time appointed by Allah ﷻ.

The word قَرْيَةٍ can mean a town or settlement, but it also refers to the people of that place and their way of life. The destruction doesn’t come randomly or prematurely. There is a fixed schedule, known to Allah ﷻ, that determines when accountability arrives.

Ayah 5

مَّا تَسْبِقُ مِنْ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلَهَا وَمَا يَسْتَـْٔخِرُونَ

No nation can ever outpace its appointed time, nor can they delay it.

This ayah reinforces the absolute precision of divine timing. تَسْبِقُ comes from the root س ب ق, which means to race ahead or win a race. No nation, no matter what it does, can cause its deadline to come earlier than appointed.

Someone might think: if these people increase in their evil and transgression, won’t punishment come sooner? The answer is no. The appointed time remains fixed. Similarly, يَسْتَـْٔخِرُونَ - they cannot push it back or delay it even slightly.

This is both a warning and a reassurance. For the evildoers, there’s no escape. For the believers watching injustice unfold, there’s the knowledge that divine justice operates on its own perfect schedule, undisturbed by human actions.

Ayah 6

وَقَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِى نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ ٱلذِّكْرُ إِنَّكَ لَمَجْنُونٌ

And they said: “O you upon whom the reminder has been sent down, you are surely insane.”

Allah ﷻ now records the offensive mockery directed at the Prophet ﷺ. Notice the sarcastic tone: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِى نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ ٱلذِّكْرُ - “O you, the one who claims the reminder has been sent down upon him.” They’re not even using his name. The contempt drips from their words.

The word ٱلذِّكْرُ - the reminder - is significant and appears multiple times in these opening passages. The Quran being called a “reminder” tells us something profound about its nature and about human beings themselves. A reminder, by definition, concerns something already known. If the Quran is a reminder from the very beginning, it means certain truths were already embedded within the human being.

We are pre-programmed with fundamental realities about our Creator, our purpose, and our accountability. The Quran came to rejuvenate and reawaken what was already placed deep within the human psyche. This is why it’s called dhikr - it reminds you of what your fitrah (natural disposition) already recognizes as true.

The word dhikr also carries the meaning of advice and counsel. Reminder and advice go hand in hand - reminding someone often means advising them of what they should be doing. The disbelievers grew tired of this constant reminder and advice, so they resorted to personal attacks: إِنَّكَ لَمَجْنُونٌ - you are surely insane.

The word مَجْنُونٌ has layered meanings. On one level, it means someone whose intellect has been covered up or overshadowed. On another level, it means someone possessed by jinn. Some people even felt pity for the Prophet ﷺ, thinking this intelligent man had lost his mind. Some relatives approached him offering to find healing, as if dealing with a medical condition.

This type of accusation is characteristic of the early Makkan period, when the disbelievers were still processing this new message and trying to explain away its power.

Ayah 7

لَّوْ مَا تَأْتِينَا بِٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ

“Why do you not bring us the angels, if you are truly among the truthful?”

After the personal attack comes the challenge. لَّوْ مَا- “how come” or “why don’t you” - expresses their demand with an air of smugness. تَأْتِينَا بِٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ - bring us the angels. Make them visible to us.

The logic they’re presenting is: You claim to see Angel Jibreel (AS) and receive revelation from him. If that’s true, why can’t we see these angels? If you’re really من الصَّـٰدِقِينَ- from among those who speak the truth - then prove it by bringing the angels before us.

This demand reveals their fundamental misunderstanding of how divine revelation works and their arrogant assumption that they can set the terms for belief.

Ayah 8

مَا نُنَزِّلُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ وَمَا كَانُوٓا۟ إِذًا مُّنظَرِينَ

We do not send down the angels except with the ultimate truth, and then they would not be given any respite.

Allah ﷻ responds directly to their challenge. مَا نُنَزِّلُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ - We don’t send down angels except with the haqq, the ultimate purpose and truth. When angels descend to earth in visible form, it’s not for entertainment or to satisfy curiosity. It’s for serious divine business, and often that business is destruction and accountability.

The second part of the ayah delivers the sobering reality: وَمَا كَانُوٓا۟ إِذًا مُّنظَرِينَ - and then they would not be given any respite. The word مُّنظَرِينَ means to be given time to wait or delay. Once the angels come in the manner these people are requesting, there will be no opportunity to reconsider, no chance to repent, no extension of the deadline.

They think they’re asking for proof, but they’re actually asking for their own destruction. Be careful what you demand as evidence, because some forms of evidence come with consequences you cannot escape.

Ayah 9

إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا ٱلذِّكْرَ وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ لَحَـٰفِظُونَ

Indeed, it is We who sent down the reminder, and indeed, We will be its guardians.

This is the second time dhikr (the reminder) is mentioned in this passage. Earlier, the disbelievers mockingly said, “O you upon whom the dhikr has been sent down, you’re insane - possessed by jinn, and jinn are giving you this so-called reminder.”

Allah ﷻ responds with absolute clarity: إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا ٱلذِّكْرَ - Indeed, it is We - using the royal We and the emphatic pronoun نَحْنُ - who sent down this dhikr. Not jinn. Not the imagination of the Prophet ﷺ. We, Allah ﷻ, are the source.

Then comes the eternal promise: وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ لَحَـٰفِظُونَ - and indeed, We will be its guardians. The word لَحَـٰفِظُونَ comes from the root ح ف ظ which means to protect, preserve, and memorize. A hafidh is someone who has memorized the Quran, and that word choice is inspired by this very ayah.

This protection of the Quran manifests in multiple ways, but its primary protection has always been through memorization and preservation in the hearts of believers. While there have been various criticisms about manuscripts, missing parchments, or the compilation process, the original and most secure protection of the Quran was never paper-based. It has always been في الصدور (in the chests) - in memorization and hifdh.

When we dedicate ourselves and our children to memorizing the Quran, we become part of the miracle of the Quran itself. We have the honor of continuing the fulfillment of this divine promise: وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ لَحَـٰفِظُونَ.

Ayah 10

وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ فِى شِيَعِ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ

And We had certainly sent messengers before you among the sects of former peoples.

Allah ﷻ now offers consolation and perspective to the Prophet ﷺ. وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ - We had already sent messengers much before you. This isn’t new. Rejection isn’t new. Mockery isn’t new.

The word شِيَعِ is particularly interesting. The singular is شِيعَة, which refers to a group that you join for protection and strength - like a political party or a tightly-knit organization. It comes from the root meaning “to spread out,” indicating a group that breaks away and becomes its own entity.

The word shee’ah typically describes groups that are religiously or politically organized and fiercely protective of their interests and identity. They develop what we might call “groupthink” - a collective mentality that becomes very difficult to penetrate from the outside.

Allah ﷻ is telling His Messenger ﷺ that the reason these people aren’t listening isn’t just individual stubbornness. They’ve formed themselves into a شِيعَة - a cohesive group with a cult-like mentality protecting their way of life and thought. But this too is nothing new. There have been countless such groups throughout history - شِيَعِ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ - among the earliest nations and peoples.

And We sent Our messengers even to the most hardheaded of these groups.

Ayah 11

وَمَا يَأْتِيهِم مِّن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ

And no messenger would come to them except that they used to ridicule him.

This ayah completes the pattern. وَمَا يَأْتِيهِم مِّن رَّسُولٍ - no messenger ever came to them - the من (min) here emphasizes “not a single one.” إِلَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ - except that they would mock him and ridicule him.

The word يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ means to make someone an object of ridicule, to mark them for mockery. This was the consistent response throughout history. Every messenger faced this same treatment.

This serves as profound comfort for the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. You’re not facing something unprecedented. You’re walking the path of all the noble messengers before you. Their people mocked them too, ridiculed them, called them insane, demanded impossible signs. And yet, the message of those prophets survived, and the mockery of their people became a cautionary tale for all future generations.

The very fact that we’re reading these ayahs today, over 1400 years later, while the names of those mockers have been forgotten, is itself a testimony to who was truly on the side of truth.

Ayah 12–25 · linked video: 02. Al-Hijr (Ayah 12-25)

Surah Al-Hijr: Ayahs 12-25

Ayah 12

كَذَٰلِكَ نَسْلُكُهُۥ فِى قُلُوبِ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ

This is how We insert it into the hearts of the criminals. The word نَسْلُكُهُ carries a powerful image - to inject something, to pierce something through something else. Think of threading beads onto a string to make a necklace, or putting your hand inside your pocket. The verb سَلَكَ conveys this idea of inserting something deep within.

What exactly is being inserted? The mockery of the messengers. The disbelief. The rejection. When this attitude becomes so deeply embedded in the hearts of those who commit crimes against the truth, reasoning no longer matters. Logic has no effect. The decision has been sealed at the deepest level of their being, and Allah ﷻ allows this as a consequence of their persistent rejection.

Ayah 13

لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِۦ ۖ وَقَدْ خَلَتْ سُنَّةُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ

They will not believe in it. This is the natural result of what was described in the previous ayah. Once rejection becomes embedded in the heart, faith cannot enter. And the traditions, the legacies, the patterns of the earliest nations have already passed. History has shown us this pattern repeatedly - communities that rejected their messengers faced consequences, and those patterns continue.

Ayah 14-15

وَلَوْ فَتَحْنَا عَلَيْهِم بَابًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَظَلُّوا۟ فِيهِ يَعْرُجُونَ

لَقَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَا سُكِّرَتْ أَبْصَـٰرُنَا بَلْ نَحْنُ قَوْمٌ مَّسْحُورُونَ

These ayahs present a striking hypothetical scenario. Even if We opened for them a door in the sky, and they spent the entire day ascending through it - فَظَلُّوا۟ indicates prolonged duration, remaining in that state all day long as they rise higher and higher - they would still deny what they witness.

The word ظَلُّوا comes from ظِلّ, which lasts a long time but isn’t permanent. This gives us the image of spending the whole day traveling upward into the heavens, having ample time to comprehend what’s happening. Yet even with such an undeniable experience, they would say: “Our eyes have been made drunk. Rather, we are a people who have been bewitched.”

This reveals a profound truth about stubborn rejection. Allah ﷻ is essentially saying: I’ve given you the Quran. They claim you’re insane, then they demand angels, then they want other miracles. But even if I gave them an elevator into the sky and they spent an entire day ascending, they would still rationalize it away as magic or illusion. There’s no point in giving them what they demand because their rejection isn’t based on lack of evidence - it’s a choice embedded in their hearts.

Ayah 16

وَلَقَدْ جَعَلْنَا فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ بُرُوجًا وَزَيَّنَّـٰهَا لِلنَّـٰظِرِينَ

And We have placed in the sky بُرُوجًا - towers, constellations, structures. The word بُرْج refers to a tower that’s visible from a distance, dome-shaped rather than pointed. These celestial structures are not random - We beautified them for those who observe, for those who actually look with open eyes and hearts.

Ayah 17-18

وَحَفِظْنَـٰهَا مِن كُلِّ شَيْطَـٰنٍ رَّجِيمٍ

إِلَّا مَنِ ٱسْتَرَقَ ٱلسَّمْعَ فَأَتْبَعَهُۥ شِهَابٌ مُّبِينٌ

We protected it from every شَيْطَـٰنٍ رَّجِيمٍ - every accursed shaytan. The word رَجِيمٍ comes from رَجْم, to throw stones at, and historically refers to someone who is cursed and pelted with stones.

These ayahs reveal something remarkable about the unseen realm. Revelation comes from the heavens, and angels carry classified information between the heavens and earth. They discuss matters unknown to us. But there’s another creation in the sky - beings made of fire, the jinn. The Arabic words for light (نُور) and fire (نَار) are linguistically close, reflecting the reality that both angels and jinn have access to the celestial realm, and there’s interaction between these two types of creation. Remember that Iblis was among the jinn but had status among the angels.

So when angels are delivering their messages, some jinn attempt to eavesdrop, to steal bits of this celestial information - إِلَّا مَنِ ٱسْتَرَقَ ٱلسَّمْعَ - except the one who steals a hearing. Like someone trying to catch office gossip from their cubicle, they might catch fragments of conversation. But the moment this theft occurs, شِهَابٌ مُّبِينٌ- a clear, brilliant flame, a shooting star - is sent after them.

You might wonder: if jinn are made of fire, how can fire hurt fire? But consider - you’re made from earth, aren’t you? Does throwing dirt in your eyes hurt? Of course it does, because you’ve become something else through the creative process. The jinn, though made from fire, have become a distinct creation that can indeed be harmed by the flames sent after them.

This is the reality behind astrology, fortune-telling, and claims of contacting spirits. Some fortune-tellers simply fabricate their predictions. Others have accessed information stolen by shayatin who risked being struck by these celestial flames to bring back fragments of knowledge. They whisper these scraps to their human followers who then claim supernatural insight.

Ayah 19

وَٱلْأَرْضَ مَدَدْنَـٰهَا وَأَلْقَيْنَا فِيهَا رَوَٰسِىَ وَأَنۢبَتْنَا فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ شَىْءٍ مَّوْزُونٍ

After describing the heavens, Allah ﷻ turns our attention to the earth. And the earth - We have stretched it out, spread it. We cast upon it رَوَٰسِىَ - mountains that are firmly pegged down, anchoring the earth. And We caused to grow in it from every thing مَّوْزُونٍ - balanced, in measure, weighed.

This word مَّوْزُونٍ is profound. It comes from the same root as مِيزَان- scale, balance. What this indicates is the delicate ecological balance that sustains life. Consider: there are countless species of fish, plants, insects, animals. If one species becomes too dominant, it can destroy the entire ecosystem. Weeds, if left unchecked, prevent other plants from growing. Certain fish species, if they multiply excessively, eliminate other species and devastate their environment.

Allah ﷻ has created an interdependent system where populations are naturally controlled, where species coexist in measured proportions. Every element of creation exists in its proper balance - مَّوْزُونٍ.

Ayah 20

وَجَعَلْنَا لَكُمْ فِيهَا مَعَـٰيِشَ وَمَن لَّسْتُمْ لَهُۥ بِرَٰزِقِينَ

And We placed for you in it مَعَـٰيِشَ - means of living, sustenance, livelihoods. But the ayah continues with something remarkable: وَمَن لَّسْتُمْ لَهُۥ بِرَٰزِقِينَ - and those for whom you are not providers.

Think about this. You might own a horse and be responsible for feeding and caring for it. But there are countless other creatures - animals, insects, birds - that you don’t feed, yet they’re being provided for. You have family members you’re responsible for feeding, but there are billions more people in the world being sustained, and who is providing for them? Who is providing for you? This ayah opens our eyes to the vast reality of Allah’s provision extending far beyond our limited sphere of responsibility.

Ayah 21

وَإِن مِّن شَىْءٍ إِلَّا عِندَنَا خَزَآئِنُهُۥ وَمَا نُنَزِّلُهُۥٓ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَّعْلُومٍ

There is not a thing except that its treasures are with Us. The full resources, the complete reserves of everything in existence - they’re all with Allah ﷻ. And We don’t send it down except بِقَدَرٍ مَّعْلُومٍ - in a known measure, a precise quantity.

Imagine a child asking for the entire jar of chocolates. A wise parent gives them just one or two - enough for their need, not so much that it harms them. Allah ﷻ owns all the water in the sky, but He sends rain in measured amounts. If He opened the floodgates completely, the world would drown. He sends heat and cold, clouds and sun, all in precise quantities.

The resources of the earth are vast but limited, and Allah ﷻ alone knows the exact measure we need at every moment. This is divine wisdom in distribution.

Ayah 22

وَأَرْسَلْنَا ٱلرِّيَـٰحَ لَوَٰقِحَ فَأَنزَلْنَا مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَأَسْقَيْنَـٰكُمُوهُ وَمَآ أَنتُمْ لَهُۥ بِخَـٰزِنِينَ

We sent the winds لَوَٰقِحَ. This is a beautiful word - it comes from لَقَحَ, which describes a pregnant she-camel carrying her baby. The winds are pregnant. They’re carrying something vital within them.

The winds carry clouds heavy with rain. They carry pollen from one territory to another, enabling plant reproduction and the continuation of life. They’re loaded with the very stuff of life, transporting it across the earth. And then We sent down water from the sky, فَأَسْقَيْنَـٰكُمُوهُ - and We gave you drink from it.

But then comes the humbling reminder: وَمَآ أَنتُمْ لَهُۥ بِخَـٰزِنِينَ - and you are not its storekeepers. You’re not the ones preserving the water. You don’t maintain the ice caps that melt each year, feeding the oceans, which feed the rivers, which supply your water. You don’t make it snow again so those caps replenish and the entire water cycle continues.

The more we learn about Earth’s water systems - the polar ice caps, the glaciers, the mountains that store frozen water - the more magnificent Allah’s design becomes. Having traveled across the Himalayas and witnessed glaciers firsthand, seeing massive blocks of ice covered in dirt, watching tiny streams of meltwater that eventually feed great rivers, standing above the clouds and seeing that thin thread of water that sustains life thousands of feet below - it’s humbling beyond words. You’re not storing any of this. Allah ﷻ is the ultimate Storekeeper of all that sustains life.

Ayah 23

وَإِنَّا لَنَحْنُ نُحْىِۦ وَنُمِيتُ وَنَحْنُ ٱلْوَٰرِثُونَ

And indeed, We - We give life and We cause death, and We are the inheritors. After discussing water, which gives life to everything, Allah ﷻ naturally transitions to the broader reality of life and death.

The concept of inheritance (وَٱلْوَٰرِثُونَ) here is powerful. We think about leaving behind our belongings - our house, car, money, possessions - to our children, who will leave them to their children, and so on. But inheritance extends beyond human transactions. The apple you don’t finish gets thrown away, and a squirrel inherits the rest of it. That squirrel dies, and insects inherit its body, extracting nutrients. Those insects die, and the earth inherits their organic matter. A plant grows from those nutrients, and we inherit that plant for food.

This chain of inheritance seems endless, passing from one creation to another. But Allah ﷻ declares: I am the ultimate Inheritor. At the end of all things, nothing will be passed down to anyone else. Everything returns to Him.

Ayah 24

وَلَقَدْ عَلِمْنَا ٱلْمُسْتَقْدِمِينَ مِنكُمْ وَلَقَدْ عَلِمْنَا ٱلْمُسْتَـْٔخِرِينَ

And We have already known those who advance among you, and We have already known those who lag behind. Allah ﷻ knows ٱلْمُسْتَقْدِمِينَ- those who are eager to move forward, those striving to get ahead in good deeds, those who want to race toward meeting their Lord. And He knows ٱلْمُسْتَـْٔخِرِينَ- those who want to delay, who feel they have more time, who aren’t ready to change yet, who want to put off their meeting with Allah ﷻ.

Both types of people are fully known to Him.

Ayah 25

وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ حَكِيمٌ عَلِيمٌ

And indeed, your Lord - He will gather them all. There’s no doubt about this. Every single one, those who rushed forward and those who held back, will be assembled before Him.

The ayah concludes with two beautiful Names: إِنَّهُۥ حَكِيمٌ عَلِيمٌ - Indeed, He is All-Wise, All-Knowing. Notice the sequence - حَكِيمٌ comes before عَلِيمٌ. His wisdom governs His knowledge. Everything He knows, He acts upon with perfect wisdom. When He gathers everyone, it will be with complete knowledge of what each person did, and with perfect wisdom in how He judges them.

Ayah 26–47 · linked video: 03. Al-Hijr (Ayah 26-47)

Surah Al-Hijr: Ayahs 26-47

Ayah 26

وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مِن صَلْصَـٰلٍ مِّنْ حَمَإٍ مَّسْنُونٍ

“And We have certainly created the human being from dried clay made from altered black mud.”

Allah ﷻ now shifts our attention to the ingredients of human creation. The Quran presents what we might call the evolutionary biology of human dirt—the stages through which the material of Adam’s (AS) creation passed before becoming worthy of receiving the divine breath.

The progression begins with تُرَاب, dried-up dirt. When turab mixes with water, it becomes طِين, wet soil—the moist dirt found beneath the grass in your backyard. Next comes طِين لَازِب, clay that has developed organic complexity, containing bacteria and other compounds. This evolves into حَمَأ , mud so dark it appears almost black, with a distinct stench from the heat and decomposition occurring within it.

The term مَسْنُون comes from the root word that gives us سُنَّة, meaning a practice or trend established over time. Water left in this state of mud for an extended period develops a sunnah—it has “the trend of being that way”—meaning it has rotted and matured through long exposure. This describes boiling hot, smelly mud that has been left to sit for ages.

Finally, when the heat causes all moisture to evaporate, the mud becomes صَلْصَال dried clay that has hardened to the consistency of pottery. Some stages later, this becomes صَلْصَال كَالْفَخَّار, dried clay like mature pottery. These represent the stages of human creation’s material origin.

This should inspire us to become serious students of creation science. Understanding the origins of creation is not forbidden knowledge—in fact, Allah ﷻ commands us: انظُرْ كَيْفَ بَدَأَ الْخَلْقَ “Look how the creation began.” This is an Islamic endeavor that must be undertaken with rigor and depth. If we abandon this study, others will pursue it and arrive at misguided conclusions that will ultimately confuse the believers—which is already happening in our time.

Ayah 27

وَٱلْجَآنَّ خَلَقْنَـٰهُ مِن قَبْلُ مِن نَّارِ ٱلسَّمُومِ

“And the jinn We created before from the fire of a scorching wind.”

The jinn were created much earlier than humans, fashioned from an entirely different substance. The word سَمُوم describes a wind so burning hot that when it touches the skin, it feels as though poison has entered the body. This scorching heat represents the original ingredient in the creation of the jinn.

However, just as we are no longer mud—having been transformed into something else from that initial ingredient—the jinn are not literally fire anymore. That was simply their starting material, which Allah ﷻ used to fashion them into their current form.

Ayah 28

وَإِذْ قَالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ إِنِّى خَـٰلِقٌۢ بَشَرًا مِّن صَلْصَـٰلٍ مِّنْ حَمَإٍ مَّسْنُونٍ

“And when your Master said to the angels, ‘Indeed, I am going to create a human being from dried clay made from altered black mud.’”

The word بَشَر literally refers to the skin of the human being—the part of our creation closest to dirt in its texture, color and nature. Allah ﷻ uses this term to emphasize our earthly origins, highlighting that our skin is the most direct connection to the clay from which we were formed.

Notice that these ingredients—the dried clay from smelly, aged mud—will continue to be repeated throughout this passage, reminding us again and again of humanity’s humble material beginning.

Ayah 29

فَإِذَا سَوَّيْتُهُۥ وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِن رُّوحِى فَقَعُوا۟ لَهُۥ سَـٰجِدِينَ

“So when I have fashioned him completely and breathed into him from My spirit, then fall down in prostration to him.”

The verb سَوَّيْتُ means to fashion or balance something with perfect proportion—fingers placed symmetrically on both hands, eyes positioned equally, everything molded and aligned with precision. Allah ﷻ announces that once He has completed this fashioning and blown into Adam (AS) from His spirit, the angels must immediately prostrate.

The timing here is critical: Allah ﷻ issued this command before the creation was completed. He told the angels, “I am going to create someone, and when I finish and breathe My spirit into him, then make prostration.” The memo was sent in advance. This means Iblis already knew what was coming—he cannot claim ignorance or surprise.

Furthermore, the command itself reveals something profound about human dignity. The dirt alone was not worthy of such honor, but once the spirit was breathed into it—فَإِذَا سَوَّيْتُهُ وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِن رُّوحِي “when I have fashioned him and breathed into him from My spirit”—then this creation became qualified for prostration. Something transformative happened, elevating the human being to a special status, making him the best of creation (أَحْسَنُ تَقْوِيم).

Ayah 30

فَسَجَدَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ كُلُّهُمْ أَجْمَعُونَ

“So the angels prostrated—all of them entirely.”

Allah ﷻ uses emphatic language here: كُلُّهُمْ (all of them) followed immediately by أَجْمَعُونَ (altogether). This double emphasis—“all of them, all of them together”—serves a specific purpose. Before we even reach the next ayah mentioning Iblis, Allah ﷻ has already resolved a potential theological confusion: every single angel prostrated without exception.

This makes it impossible to consider Iblis an angel. The phrase كُلُّهُمْ أَجْمَعُونَ leaves no room for any angel to be excluded. One word of emphasis would have been sufficient, but Allah ﷻ provides two layers of confirmation, closing the door on misunderstanding before the exception is even mentioned.

Ayah 31

إِلَّآ إِبْلِيسَ أَبَىٰٓ أَن يَكُونَ مَعَ ٱلسَّـٰجِدِينَ

“Except Iblis; he refused to be among those who prostrated.”

The verb أَبَىٰ means to refuse with determination and persistence. Iblis did not simply neglect the command or forget—he actively refused to join those in prostration.

Ayah 32

قَالَ يَـٰٓإِبْلِيسُ مَا لَكَ أَلَّا تَكُونَ مَعَ ٱلسَّـٰجِدِينَ

“He said, ‘O Iblis, what is wrong with you that you should not be among those who prostrate?’”

Allah ﷻ confronts Iblis directly: “What is wrong with you?” This is not a question seeking information—Allah ﷻ knows the answer—but rather a public declaration, forcing Iblis to reveal the corruption that has been festering in his heart.

Ayah 33

قَالَ لَمْ أَكُن لِّأَسْجُدَ لِبَشَرٍ خَلَقْتَهُۥ مِن صَلْصَـٰلٍ مِّنْ حَمَإٍ مَّسْنُونٍ

“He said, ‘Never would I prostrate to a human being whom You created from dried clay made from altered black mud.’”

The phrase لَمْ أَكُن (“I was never”) reveals the depth of Iblis’s rebellion. He is saying, “From the very moment You informed us this day would come, I had already made up my mind. I was never going to do this.” His refusal was premeditated, not impulsive.

His argument centers on the material origin: “You made him from smelly mud that dried up. You told us this Yourself. You want me to prostrate to him?” He uses Allah’s own description of the creation process as justification for his disobedience.

This is why, in Surah Al-Baqarah, Allah ﷻ describes him with the phrase وَكَانَ مِنَ الْكَافِرِينَ “and he was of the disbelievers”—not “became” (أَصْبَحَ) but “was” (كَانَ). His disbelief existed all along, hidden in his heart. It simply became obvious to everyone else at this moment, but the corruption had been there from the beginning.

Ayah 34

قَالَ فَٱخْرُجْ مِنْهَا فَإِنَّكَ رَجِيمٌ

“He said, ‘Then get out of it, for indeed you are expelled.’”

The word رَجِيم means one who is stoned, cursed, cast out with violence. Allah ﷻ issues the immediate verdict: expulsion from this honored place.

Ayah 35

وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ ٱللَّعْنَةَ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ

“And indeed, upon you specifically is the curse until the Day of Judgment.”

The phrase عَلَيْكَ (upon you specifically) emphasizes that this curse targets Iblis in particular. The curse will remain on him continuously until the Day of Judgment—no reprieve, no forgiveness, no second chance.

Ayah 36

قَالَ رَبِّ فَأَنظِرْنِىٓ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ

“He said, ‘My Master, then grant me respite until the Day they are resurrected.’”

Notice that Iblis still calls Allah “رَبِّ” (my Master). People often overlook this detail. He is not denying Allah’s lordship—that level of denial belongs to human beings, not to shaytan. Iblis acknowledges Allah ﷻ as his Master even while speaking to Him in a manner completely unbecoming of a slave. You can accept Allah’s lordship and still speak about Him or behave toward His commands in ways that are fundamentally rebellious.

Ayah 37-38

قَالَ فَإِنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُنظَرِينَ - إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْوَقْتِ ٱلْمَعْلُومِ

“He said, ‘Then indeed, you are of those granted respite—until the Day of the known time.’”

Allah ﷻ grants the request, but with a specification: until يَوْمِ الْوَقْتِ الْمَعْلُومِ, the Day of the known time. The phrasing reveals something subtle: while we say “the time is not known” because humans don’t know when the Hour will occur, Allah ﷻ describes it as “known.” To Him, it is معلوم—its signs are known, its events are known, and of course, the exact timing is known to Him alone.

The plural form المُنظَرِينَ (those granted respite) suggests Iblis may not be alone—there may be other shayateen who have been given this same extended time alongside him.

Ayah 39

قَالَ رَبِّ بِمَآ أَغْوَيْتَنِى لَأُزَيِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ

“He said, ‘My Master, because You have caused me to err, I will surely make the earth appear attractive to them and I will mislead them all.’”

Now we see a new dimension of Iblis’s strategy. In Surah Al-A’raf, we learned that he would يَقْعُدَنَّ لَهُمْ صِرَاطَكَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ “sit in wait for them on Your straight path” (7:16). But here he reveals a different tactic altogether: لَأُزَيِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ “I will beautify for them in the earth.”

Allah ﷻ Himself says in the Quran إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا مَا عَلَى الْأَرْضِ زِينَةً لَّهَا “Indeed, We have made what is on the earth an adornment for it.” He made the world beautiful. Now Iblis declares, “I will make the world beautiful for them”—but with a twisted purpose.

He will make earthly pleasures, material possessions, status and comfort appear so overwhelmingly attractive that people become obsessed. They will see beauty nowhere else. Their focus will be consumed entirely by the adornments of this temporary world, and whatever they pursue in the earth, he will beautify it for them until they cannot see beyond it.

The phrase وَلَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ means “I will mislead them far, far away”—taking them so distant from the truth that they cannot find their way back. He intends to drop the standard so low and take entire populations so far off course that return becomes nearly impossible.

Ayah 40

إِلَّا عِبَادَكَ مِنْهُمُ ٱلْمُخْلَصِينَ

“Except Your sincere slaves among them.”

Even in his arrogance, Iblis acknowledges his limitation. He admits, “The slaves among them—those whom You have granted sincerity through Your divine intervention—I will have no power over them.”

The word المُخْلَصِين is the passive form, meaning “those made sincere.” This differs from المُخْلِصِين, the active form meaning “those who are sincere.” The passive form indicates that these are people whom Allah ﷻ has granted the gift of sincerity, people who have received divine help in maintaining their purity and devotion. They did not achieve this entirely on their own—Allah ﷻ intervened to protect them.

Ayah 41

قَالَ هَـٰذَا صِرَٰطٌ عَلَىَّ مُسْتَقِيمٌ

“He said, ‘This is a path leading straight to Me.’”

The phrase صِرَاطٌ عَلَيَّ مُسْتَقِيمٌ can be understood in multiple ways. عَلَيَّ could mean “mandated upon Me”—that showing this straight path is a responsibility Allah ﷻ has taken upon Himself. It could also carry the meaning of إِلَيَّ, “leading to Me”—this path leads directly to Allah ﷻ. Additionally, عَلَى can indicate elevation, suggesting “you will be raised up on this path.”

All these meanings converge: this is a path Allah ﷻ has obligated Himself to show, it leads to Him, and it elevates those who walk upon it.

Ayah 42

إِنَّ عِبَادِى لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَـٰنٌ إِلَّا مَنِ ٱتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ ٱلْغَاوِينَ

“Indeed, My slaves—you will have no authority over them at all, except for those who follow you from among those who go astray.”

Allah ﷻ makes an absolute declaration: لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَانٌ “you will have no authority, no power, no course of influence against them whatsoever.” The word سُلْطَان indicates complete lack of control.

However, there is an exception: إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغَاوِينَ “except those who follow you from among those who get lost.” The word الْغَاوِين has appeared repeatedly in this passage. It implies people who wander off the path. Allah’s slaves will remain on the straight path, continuously fighting shaytan, but sometimes they might slip—and in those moments of weakness, shaytan can gain temporary influence. But even then, they are not permanently under his authority; they can return to the path.

Ayah 43

وَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمَوْعِدُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ

“And indeed, Hell is the promised place for them all.”

For those who do follow Iblis and persist in their deviation, their final destination is clear: Jahannam is their موعد, their appointed meeting place.

Ayah 44

لَهَا سَبْعَةُ أَبْوَٰبٍ لِّكُلِّ بَابٍ مِّنْهُمْ جُزْءٌ مَّقْسُومٌ

“It has seven gates; for every gate is a portion of them assigned.”

Hell is described as having seven gates, and for each gate, a specific portion of people has been divided and designated. Perhaps certain types of sins lead through one gate, while other sins lead through another. The deniers of one messenger might enter through one gate, while the deniers of another messenger enter through a different one. Each entrance has its own population allocated to it, organized according to Allah’s perfect justice and wisdom.

Ayah 45

إِنَّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ وَعُيُونٍ

“Indeed, the righteous will be in gardens and springs.”

After the sobering description of Hell, Allah ﷻ immediately shifts to the contrasting destination: إِنَّ الْمُتَّقِينَ فِي جَنَّاتٍ وَعُيُونٍ “Indeed, the people who protected themselves, who were cautious and careful throughout their lives, will be in the midst of multiple gardens and countless springs and waterfalls.”

Ayah 46

ٱدْخُلُوهَا بِسَلَـٰمٍ ءَامِنِينَ

“Enter it in peace, secure.”

This sudden shift in pronoun creates one of the most beautiful rhetorical moments in the Quran. Allah ﷻ was speaking in third person: “The people of taqwa will be in gardens.” The listener naturally thinks, “They will be there—those righteous people, not me necessarily.”

But then immediately, without warning, the address changes to second person: ادْخُلُوهَا “Enter it!” The listener’s heart catches—“Wait, He’s talking to me now? I’m included? This could be said to me?”

The original audience would have experienced this shift viscerally. They went from hearing about a distant group to suddenly being directly addressed, personally invited. It plants hope: “I could be among them. Allah ﷻ might speak these words to me.”

The phrase بِسَلَامٍ means “in peace.” Enter in a state of peace. And آمِنِينَ means “secure, safe now.” Why the emphasis on safety? Because to reach Paradise, you have to traverse dangerous territory. The journey through the Hereafter—crossing the bridge over Hell, the questioning, the accounting—all of this creates anxiety. Even after entering the gates, there might be lingering worry: “Is there more ahead? More tests? More danger?”

Allah ﷻ reassures: آمِنِينَ “You are safe now. Relax. You made it. There is nothing to fear anymore.”

Ayah 47

وَنَزَعْنَا مَا فِى صُدُورِهِم مِّنْ غِلٍّ إِخْوَٰنًا عَلَىٰ سُرُرٍ مُّتَقَـٰبِلِينَ

“And We will remove whatever is in their hearts of resentment, as brothers facing one another on thrones.”

After the personal invitation in the previous ayah, Allah ﷻ returns to third person—yet the encouragement lingers. He describes the final purification that makes Paradise truly perfect.

The word نَزَعْنَا means to pull out forcefully, to yank something out. And what is being removed? غِلّ—deep-seated resentment, ill feelings, grudges that have taken root in the heart.

This could mean that there will be brotherhood in Paradise—people will be brothers there. Or it could mean that whatever ill feelings developed between brothers in this world will be removed in the Hereafter.

Let’s be honest: just because we’re Muslims, just because we’re studying together, just because we’re working for the same cause, doesn’t mean we automatically get along perfectly all the time. It is completely normal within Islam to find certain people difficult, to feel irritation around them, to need space from them. You might have a brother in faith who, when he comes within a ten-foot radius, you start having allergic reactions. You can’t breathe normally. You need to leave. There’s just this agitation.

And you might feel guilty: “He’s Muslim—how can I feel this way?” But most of us don’t actually feel that guilty.

Sometimes we say things to each other that hurt. Those scars don’t always heal as the years pass. You maintain a kind of defensive attitude toward that person. It’s not exactly perfect, you know? Recently, I met a brother who said some truly harsh things to me a long time ago. We had lunch together this past weekend, and we were very cordial and pleasant with one another. But you can’t help but remember. It crosses your mind: “Yeah… I hope that doesn’t come up.”

Allah ﷻ knows we cannot remove these feelings ourselves. So He says وَنَزَعْنَا مَا فِي صُدُورِهِم مِّنْ غِلٍّ—He will yank it out of your heart, because He knows you are incapable of doing it on your own.

Only after this purification can we truly be إِخْوَانًا عَلَىٰ سُرُرٍ مُّتَقَابِلِينَ “brothers on couches facing one another.” Only then are you genuinely ready to face each other. Now you’re actually happy to see them. It’s not the obligatory “Assalamu alaikum, brother” while internally wishing you were somewhere else. No—you’re truly, deeply happy to be in each other’s presence.

Just because Muslims are gathered together doesn’t automatically mean they’re happy with one another. The ghil has to be removed first. Once it is, then we can finally enjoy each other’s company in the way Allah ﷻ always intended.

Ayah 48–75 · linked video: 04. Al-Hijr (Ayah 48-75)

Surah Al-Hijr: Ayahs 48-75

Ayah 48

لَا يَمَسُّهُمْ فِيهَا نَصَبٌ وَمَا هُم مِّنْهَا بِمُخْرَجِينَ

No exhaustion will ever touch them in Paradise. The word نَصَب literally means exhaustion, the kind that comes from working so hard that you can’t even stand up anymore. The root نَصَبَ means to stick something firmly into the ground, like pegging a stick or planting a flag. From this comes the meaning of exertion so intense that it plants you in place from fatigue.

This exhaustion can come even from enjoyment. We’ve all experienced gatherings, parties or celebrations where eventually we have to excuse ourselves because we’re completely drained. Our eyes start shutting, we remember work the next morning, and we realize it’s already four in the morning. That familiar feeling of needing to leave because we’re simply done.

But in Paradise, لَا يَمَسُّهُمْ فِيهَا نَصَبٌ - no such exhaustion will ever touch them. The verb يَمَسُّ emphasizes that it won’t even graze them. Furthermore, وَمَا هُم مِّنْهَا بِمُخْرَجِينَ - they will never be expelled from it. They won’t be removed, they won’t be taken out. The permanence of Paradise is absolute.

Ayah 49

۞ نَبِّئْ عِبَادِىٓ أَنِّىٓ أَنَا ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ

Allah ﷻ commands His Messenger ﷺ: نَبِّئْ عِبَادِىٓ - inform My slaves. Let them know something crucial about who I am. أَنِّىٓ أَنَا ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ - that I am indeed the Extremely Forgiving, the Continually Merciful.

This command comes after the mention of Hellfire and its terrors. Allah ﷻ has scared them, yes, warned them of the consequences of rejection. But now He wants them to know directly: despite all the warnings, despite the punishment that awaits the disbelievers, I am still the One who is غَفُور- extremely forgiving - and رَحِيم - always merciful. They have an opportunity. The door remains open.

Ayah 50

وَأَنَّ عَذَابِى هُوَ ٱلْعَذَابُ ٱلْأَلِيمُ

However, وَأَنَّ عَذَابِى هُوَ ٱلْعَذَابُ ٱلْأَلِيمُ - and that My punishment is indeed the painful punishment. On one hand, mercy. On the other hand, a clear warning about punishment. This is the balance that Allah ﷻ maintains throughout the Quran: hope and fear, mercy and warning, existing side by side.

Ayah 51

وَنَبِّئْهُمْ عَن ضَيْفِ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ

The transition in the Quran flows like rivers into oceans. After establishing His mercy and His punishment, Allah ﷻ immediately says: وَنَبِّئْهُمْ عَن ضَيْفِ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ - and inform them about the guests of Ibrahim (AS).

This connection is profound. Allah ﷻ just told us He is forgiving and He can punish. Now He says: tell them about the guests of Ibrahim (AS). Why this story now? Because the guests of Ibrahim (AS) came with both messages. They brought mercy - the news that he would have a child. And they brought punishment - their mission against the nation of Lut (AS). This story embodies exactly what was just stated: Allah’s mercy and Allah’s punishment, manifested in a single visitation.

Ayah 52

إِذْ دَخَلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِ فَقَالُوا۟ سَلَـٰمًا قَالَ إِنَّا مِنكُمْ وَجِلُونَ

This story has appeared before in the Quran, but now we’re seeing it from a different angle, like viewing a recording from another camera that shows us details we hadn’t seen before.

إِذْ دَخَلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِ - when they entered upon him. فَقَالُوا۟ سَلَـٰمًا - they said, “Peace.” In previous narrations, we learned that Ibrahim (AS) was hiding his fear. But now the Quran reveals that he couldn’t hide it any longer. He had to let them know.

قَالَ إِنَّا مِنكُمْ وَجِلُونَ - he said, “We are indeed afraid of you.” The word وَجِل describes internal fear, the kind you try to conceal. But Ibrahim (AS) couldn’t hide it anymore, even though he didn’t necessarily look afraid. The situation had become too unsettling, so he had to voice what he was feeling inside.

Ayah 53

قَالُوا۟ لَا تَوْجَلْ إِنَّا نُبَشِّرُكَ بِغُلَـٰمٍ عَلِيمٍ

The angels responded: قَالُوا۟ لَا تَوْجَلْ - don’t be afraid. إِنَّا نُبَشِّرُكَ بِغُلَـٰمٍ عَلِيمٍ - we give you good news of a boy who will be very knowledgeable.

Notice that these are multiple angels. You don’t need multiple angels just to deliver one piece of news - that you’re going to have a baby. Congratulations, we’re leaving. There’s clearly something more to their mission.

The word غُلَـٰم refers to a boy roughly between the ages of seven and thirteen. By describing the child as عَلِيم - extremely knowledgeable - even as a غُلَـٰم, the angels are indicating that this boy will be remarkably intelligent from a young age. This also implies something else: Ibrahim (AS) and his wife will live long enough not just to have this child, but to see him grow into his teenage years and witness his exceptional wisdom.

Ayah 54

قَالَ أَبَشَّرْتُمُونِى عَلَىٰٓ أَن مَّسَّنِىَ ٱلْكِبَرُ فَبِمَ تُبَشِّرُونَ

Ibrahim (AS) responded with astonishment: أَبَشَّرْتُمُونِى عَلَىٰٓ أَن مَّسَّنِىَ ٱلْكِبَرُ - are you giving me good news even though old age has already seized me? The verb مَسَّنِىَ means “has touched me” or “has gotten hold of me.” Old age has already taken hold.

فَبِمَ تُبَشِّرُونَ - so what are you really congratulating me about? What are you actually talking about? His question reveals his amazement at this seemingly impossible news.

Ayah 55

قَالُوا۟ بَشَّرْنَـٰكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ فَلَا تَكُن مِّنَ ٱلْقَـٰنِطِينَ

The angels reassured him: بَشَّرْنَـٰكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ - we’ve given you good news with truth, with revelation. This is real. This is from Allah ﷻ.

فَلَا تَكُن مِّنَ ٱلْقَـٰنِطِينَ - don’t become from those who lose hope altogether. The word قَـٰنِط describes someone in extreme depression, someone who has lost all hope. It’s like the person who always looks at the negative side of everything, who finds something to sour the mood no matter what. The foundation is going to crack. The weather is nice, but people still die here. That’s a قَـٰنِط - someone who can’t see the positive in anything.

The angels are telling him: don’t be like that. Don’t look at the impossibility. Look at the promise.

Ayah 56

قَالَ وَمَن يَقْنَطُ مِن رَّحْمَةِ رَبِّهِۦٓ إِلَّا ٱلضَّآلُّونَ

Ibrahim (AS) responds with clarity: وَمَن يَقْنَطُ مِن رَّحْمَةِ رَبِّهِۦٓ إِلَّا ٱلضَّآلُّونَ - and who despairs of the mercy of his Master except those who are astray, the misled?

In other words: that’s not me. I think you’ve confused me with someone else. I’m not from among الضَّآلُّونَ - the lost, the misguided. Ibrahim (AS) isn’t questioning Allah’s power or doubting His mercy. He’s simply expressing human astonishment at the miraculous nature of what he’s being told.

Ayah 57

قَالَ فَمَا خَطْبُكُمْ أَيُّهَا ٱلْمُرْسَلُونَ

But Ibrahim (AS) is exceptionally perceptive. He realizes these angels didn’t come just to tell him about a baby. Something else is happening. So he asks directly: فَمَا خَطْبُكُمْ أَيُّهَا ٱلْمُرْسَلُونَ - what is your real mission, O messengers?

The word خَطْب is specifically used for a situation involving trouble or something serious. You don’t say “what’s your خَطْب” for casual matters. When you use this word, you’re asking about something significant, often something difficult. Ibrahim (AS) is essentially asking: what are you really up to? What’s the serious matter you’ve come for?

Ayah 58

قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّآ أُرْسِلْنَآ إِلَىٰ قَوْمٍ مُّجْرِمِينَ

They revealed their true mission: إِنَّآ أُرْسِلْنَآ إِلَىٰ قَوْمٍ مُّجْرِمِينَ - we have been sent to a criminal nation.

They had wanted to stop by and tell him about the baby, about how smart his son would be. But Ibrahim (AS) wasn’t distracted by that wonderful news. He acknowledged it - thanks, that’s amazing about the baby - but he pressed on: what’s your real purpose? And now they reveal the weight of their mission.

Ayah 59

إِلَّآ ءَالَ لُوطٍ إِنَّا لَمُنَجُّوهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ

إِلَّآ ءَالَ لُوطٍ - except for the family of Lut (AS). The word آل (aal) refers to the immediate family, the household.

إِنَّا لَمُنَجُّوهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ - we are certainly going to rescue them, all of them altogether. The verb مُنَجُّوهُمْ comes from نَجَا, meaning to rescue or save. The form used here emphasizes intensity and certainty. We won’t leave any of his family behind. We’re getting them all out.

Ayah 60

إِلَّا ٱمْرَأَتَهُۥ قَدَّرْنَآ ۙ إِنَّهَا لَمِنَ ٱلْغَـٰبِرِينَ

Then comes the exception: إِلَّا ٱمْرَأَتَهُۥ - except his wife. قَدَّرْنَآ - we have estimated, we have calculated, we have concluded. The decision has been made regarding her fate.

إِنَّهَا لَمِنَ ٱلْغَـٰبِرِينَ - she will be from those who are left behind. The word غَـٰبِر describes someone trying to catch up with you but getting left behind, like a straggler who can’t keep pace. Despite being part of Lut’s (AS) household, she will be among those left behind when destruction comes.

Ayah 61-62

فَلَمَّا جَآءَ ءَالَ لُوطٍ ٱلْمُرْسَلُونَ - قَالَ إِنَّكُمْ قَوْمٌ مُّنكَرُونَ

When the messengers came to the household of Lut (AS), he opened the door and immediately said: إِنَّكُمْ قَوْمٌ مُّنكَرُونَ - you are people unknown to me. I don’t recognize you. The word مُّنكَر emphasizes that these are strangers, people he’s never seen before, which in his situation meant potential danger.

Ayah 63

قَالُوا۟ بَلْ جِئْنَـٰكَ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ فِيهِ يَمْتَرُونَ

The angels responded: بَلْ جِئْنَـٰكَ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ فِيهِ يَمْتَرُونَ - rather, we have come to you with the very thing they were in doubt about. They don’t spell it out explicitly, but the meaning is clear: we’ve come with the punishment. The very punishment that these people doubted would ever come, that they mocked and dismissed - that’s what we’ve brought.

Ayah 64

وَأَتَيْنَـٰكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَإِنَّا لَصَـٰدِقُونَ

وَأَتَيْنَـٰكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ - and we have come to you with the truth, with certainty, with purpose. وَإِنَّا لَصَـٰدِقُونَ - and we are indeed truthful. Everything we’re telling you is real. This is happening.

Ayah 65

فَأَسْرِ بِأَهْلِكَ بِقِطْعٍ مِّنَ ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱتَّبِعْ أَدْبَـٰرَهُمْ وَلَا يَلْتَفِتْ مِنكُمْ أَحَدٌ وَٱمْضُوا۟ حَيْثُ تُؤْمَرُونَ

Then came the urgent command: فَأَسْرِ بِأَهْلِكَ بِقِطْعٍ مِّنَ ٱلَّيْلِ - leave immediately with your family in a portion of the night, in the middle of the night. بِقِطْعٍ suggests a very small portion, the darkest part of night.

وَٱتَّبِعْ أَدْبَـٰرَهُمْ - and follow their backs. In other words, you should be the last to leave. If you’re following their backs, you’re seeing the backs of your family, which means you’re behind them. This places the responsibility squarely on Lut (AS): make sure everyone got out. That’s your duty as a messenger. When people need to be saved, he has to ensure they’re out first, and only then does he leave.

وَلَا يَلْتَفِتْ مِنكُمْ أَحَدٌ - and let not one of you turn around. Don’t look back. The command is emphatic and absolute.

وَٱمْضُوا۟ حَيْثُ تُؤْمَرُونَ - and keep walking where you’ve been commanded to go. The word مَضَى means to pass by, to walk past something, to let something be. It can also mean to let go, to not discuss something anymore, to just move forward. Here it means: keep walking in the direction you’ve been told. Don’t deviate, don’t stop, don’t turn around.

Imagine the terror of that walk. You can’t even look back. You start hearing unimaginable sounds behind you - the destruction beginning - but you have to keep walking forward. The angels are essentially telling them: you go that way, because the debris, the destruction, is going to follow this way. By the time we start, you need to be far enough that you’re safe.

Ayah 66

وَقَضَيْنَآ إِلَيْهِ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْأَمْرَ أَنَّ دَابِرَ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ مَقْطُوعٌ مُّصْبِحِينَ

وَقَضَيْنَآ إِلَيْهِ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْأَمْرَ - and We conveyed to him that decision, We dictated to him that matter. The word قَضَى means to decree, to finalize, to pass judgment.

أَنَّ دَابِرَ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ مَقْطُوعٌ مُّصْبِحِينَ - that the remnant of these people will be cut off by morning. The word دَابِر literally means the rear, the last part, the tail end - essentially, their strength, their backbone. By the time morning comes, when they experience the dawn, they will be completely executed, utterly destroyed.

Ayah 67

وَجَآءَ أَهْلُ ٱلْمَدِينَةِ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ

Now the scene shifts back to earlier that evening, before the night of escape. وَجَآءَ أَهْلُ ٱلْمَدِينَةِ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ - and the people of the city came, rejoicing, congratulating one another.

They had learned that handsome-looking men, strangers, were at Lut’s (AS) house. The details of their depravity have been mentioned elsewhere in the Quran, so here we get the condensed version because the context is already understood.

Ayah 68

قَالَ إِنَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ضَيْفِى فَلَا تَفْضَحُونِ

Lut (AS) pleaded with them: إِنَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ضَيْفِى - these are my guests. فَلَا تَفْضَحُونِ - don’t humiliate me, don’t disgrace me.

The word فَضَح means to humiliate someone, to expose someone’s flaws, to put somebody down before others, to publicly embarrass them. Lut (AS) is saying: don’t bring this public shame upon me. Respect the sanctity of my household and my guests.

Ayah 69

وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَلَا تُخْزُونِ

وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ - and be conscious of Allah, have some fear of Allah. وَلَا تُخْزُونِ - and don’t humiliate me, don’t bring disgrace upon me. He’s appealing to whatever sense of morality or decency might remain in them.

Ayah 70

قَالُوٓا۟ أَوَلَمْ نَنْهَكَ عَنِ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

Their response was chilling: أَوَلَمْ نَنْهَكَ عَنِ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ - didn’t we forbid you from all people? A modern translation would be: didn’t we tell you to mind your own business? Don’t meddle in everybody’s affairs.

Literally, they’re saying: didn’t we prevent you from protecting all peoples, all nations of the world? What that means is: leave the world alone. These strangers aren’t your family, are they? What are you worried about? Let us deal with them. It’s no big deal. Mind your own business.

Ayah 71

قَالَ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ بَنَاتِىٓ إِن كُنتُمْ فَـٰعِلِينَ

In desperation, Lut (AS) said: هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ بَنَاتِىٓ إِن كُنتُمْ فَـٰعِلِينَ - these are my daughters, if you must do something.

Some scholars say he was offering his own daughters in marriage to the chiefs of the tribes, asking why they had such deviant urges when lawful marriage was available. Others say, and this interpretation is more convincing, that he was pointing at the women of his community that they had abandoned. Why are you ignoring my daughters - meaning the women of the nation? A prophet is like the father of his people, so the women of his community are like his daughters.

Ayah 72

لَعَمْرُكَ إِنَّهُمْ لَفِى سَكْرَتِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ

This ayah contains one of the greatest honors bestowed upon the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ in the Quran. Allah ﷻ says: لَعَمْرُكَ - I swear by your life, by your lifespan.

This type of language is used between very close, intimate friends. Throughout the Quran, Allah ﷻ swears by many things - by the sun, by the night, by time - but here, uniquely, He swears by the life of His Messenger ﷺ. It’s an extraordinary expression of honor and closeness.

إِنَّهُمْ لَفِى سَكْرَتِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ - they were completely drunk in their intoxication, wandering blindly. The word عَمَه (amah) means to be blind, but specifically blind of heart and reason. They were so consumed by their urges, so intoxicated by their lusts, that they were completely blinded in their drunken state. There was no reasoning with these people. They had lost all sense of right and wrong, all capacity for rational thought.

Ayah 73

فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلصَّيْحَةُ مُشْرِقِينَ

فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلصَّيْحَةُ - then the blast, the mighty scream, the terrifying cry seized them. مُشْرِقِينَ - as the sun rose upon them, as they found themselves in the morning light. The word مُشْرِقِينَ comes from شَرَقَ, meaning when the sun rises and spreads its light.

The timing is precise: just as the morning light began to appear, just as the sun rose, that’s when the punishment struck.

Ayah 74

فَجَعَلْنَا عَـٰلِيَهَا سَافِلَهَا وَأَمْطَرْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ حِجَارَةً مِّن سِجِّيلٍ

فَجَعَلْنَا عَـٰلِيَهَا سَافِلَهَا - We made its high places into its low places. The ceilings, the roofs, the upper parts of their buildings became the lower parts. The structures collapsed. The high became low.

Imagine being inside a building when the ceiling starts falling apart. What would you do? You’d run outside to escape. That’s exactly what happened. Once they fled their homes to escape the collapsing buildings, وَأَمْطَرْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ حِجَارَةً مِّن سِجِّيلٍ - We rained upon them stones of baked clay.

The word سِجِّيل is a Persian loanword - سنگ گل - meaning baked clay. First, Allah ﷻ forced them out of their homes by dropping the ceilings. He made the high places low. Then, when they came outside seeking safety, the bombardment began. Stones of baked clay poured down upon them like massive gunfire, relentless and inescapable.

Ayah 75

إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّلْمُتَوَسِّمِينَ

إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ - in that are certainly miraculous signs, clear evidences, powerful lessons. لِّلْمُتَوَسِّمِينَ - for those who can read the signs.

The word مُتَوَسِّم comes from وَسْم, which means a name, a symbol, a marking. A مُتَوَسِّم is someone who can look at hints, symbols, traces and reach the correct conclusion. Like a tracker who examines the ground and says, “He went this way.” They look at the clues and make intelligent deductions.

Allah ﷻ is saying: intelligent people, those who reflect, can look at these signs and understand. Imagine a person in Makkah walking along and seeing a small pebble on the ground. After hearing this story, that single pebble is enough for him. He can make the connection: this could very well have been one of the bullets meant for me if I continue in disbelief. That rain could come again.

This is the power Allah ﷻ gives to those who reflect: the ability to see signs everywhere, to learn from the traces of history, to understand that the same God who punished the people of Lut (AS) is the same God who watches over us today. The choice we make in response to His message will determine whether we receive His mercy or His punishment.

Ayah 76–99 · linked video: 05. Al-Hijr (Ayah 76-99)

Surah Al-Hijr: Ayahs 76-99

Ayah 76

وَإِنَّهَا لَبِسَبِيلٍ مُّقِيمٍ

This ayah refers back to the destroyed town of the people of Lut (AS) - commonly known as Sodom and Gomorrah. Allah ﷻ tells us that these ruins lie لَبِسَبِيلٍ مُّقِيمٍ (upon an established road). The word سَبِيل refers to an easily traveled or popularly traveled road.

To understand the significance of this location, we need to recognize the geography of ancient trade routes. Before the Suez Canal existed, trade between the East and West followed a specific path. Goods from China and India would arrive at the ports in Yemen, while trade from the Roman Empire came to ports in Syria. The desert route connecting these two major trade hubs passed directly through the lands where these destroyed nations once stood.

The people of Madyan, the people of al-Aikah, and other destroyed civilizations left their ruins along this well-traveled highway. Every caravan passing through would witness these remnants of divine punishment. The Quraysh themselves, as a merchant community, would have traveled this route regularly and seen these ruins with their own eyes.

Ayah 77

إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

Allah ﷻ declares that in these ruins lies a miraculous sign - but specifically لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (for those who truly believe). The destruction isn’t just ancient history; it’s a living testimony available for anyone traveling that route to witness and reflect upon. These physical remnants serve as tangible evidence of Allah’s promise that truth will prevail and wrongdoing will be punished.

Ayah 78

وَإِن كَانَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْأَيْكَةِ لَظَـٰلِمِينَ

Now Allah ﷻ introduces another destroyed nation: أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْأَيْكَةِ (the people of al-Aikah). Al-Aikah refers to the giant tree that this nation used to worship. These were the people of Prophet Shu’ayb (AS), and they were truly wrongdoers who persisted in their oppression and rejection of the truth.

Ayah 79

فَٱنتَقَمْنَا مِنْهُمْ وَإِنَّهُمَا لَبِإِمَامٍ مُّبِينٍ

After establishing their wrongdoing, Allah ﷻ declares: فَٱنتَقَمْنَا مِنْهُمْ (then We avenged from them - We took revenge from them). The ayah then states that both of these nations - the people of Lut (AS) and the people of al-Aikah - remain لَبِإِمَامٍ مُّبِينٍ (on an open highway).

Notice the progression in terminology: first Allah ﷻ used سَبِيل (road), and now He uses إِمَام (highway). This refers to the more stretched, expansive parts of the road where these ruins are visible to travelers.

Ayah 80

وَلَقَدْ كَذَّبَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْحِجْرِ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ

Allah ﷻ now mentions أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْحِجْرِ (the people of al-Hijr), which is another name for the nation of Thamud, the people of Prophet Salih (AS). Just as the nation of ’Ad was also called أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْأَحْقَافِ (the people of al-Ahqaf) after their region, Thamud is called the people of al-Hijr after their land.

The ayah states they كَذَّبَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (lied against those who were sent). This phrase carries multiple meanings. When you deny one messenger, in essence you deny all messengers, because the core message is the same. Some scholars also suggest that perhaps multiple messengers were sent to them, and they rejected them all. The plural form indicates the gravity of their rejection.

Ayah 81

وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا فَكَانُوا۟ عَنْهَا مُعْرِضِينَ

Despite receiving clear signs - وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا (We sent Our miraculous signs to them) - their response was persistent rejection. The phrase فَكَانُوا۟ عَنْهَا مُعْرِضِينَ (they used to be oblivious to them, deliberately ignoring them) indicates this wasn’t a one-time rejection but a continuous pattern of turning away from the truth.

Ayah 82

وَكَانُوا۟ يَنْحِتُونَ مِنَ ٱلْجِبَالِ بُيُوتًا ءَامِنِينَ

This ayah describes their material prowess: يَنْحِتُونَ مِنَ ٱلْجِبَالِ بُيُوتًا (they used to carve out of mountains homes). They carved dwellings directly from rock, creating sophisticated architecture that made them feel ءَامِنِينَ (secure). They lived in these mountain homes with a sense of safety and permanence.

Remarkably, many of these structures remain visible today. If you travel to the region of al-Hijr, you can still see these ancient ruins carved into mountains. The advanced architecture they achieved stands as a testament to their civilization’s capabilities.

Ayah 83

فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلصَّيْحَةُ مُصْبِحِينَ

Despite their architectural achievements and sense of security, فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلصَّيْحَةُ (the loud explosion took them). The punishment came مُصْبِحِينَ (by morning time), suddenly and completely.

Ayah 84

فَمَآ أَغْنَىٰ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ

All their earnings, all their material achievements - مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ (whatever they used to earn) - brought them no benefit when destruction came. Their sophisticated homes carved from mountains, their wealth, their security - none of it could save them from Allah’s punishment. Nothing they had accumulated came to their aid or freed them from the destruction they faced.

Ayah 85

وَمَا خَلَقْنَا ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَآ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ ۗ وَإِنَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ لَـَٔاتِيَةٌ ۖ فَٱصْفَحِ ٱلصَّفْحَ ٱلْجَمِيلَ

This ayah marks a powerful transition in the surah. After recounting the destruction of previous nations, Allah ﷻ declares a universal truth: وَمَا خَلَقْنَا ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَآ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ (We didn’t create the skies and the earth and whatever lies between them except with purpose).

This directly challenges certain philosophical and religious views that see creation as purposeless. Some aspects of ancient philosophies suggested that God plays with creation like a child with toys - causing earthquakes or disasters on a whim. Modern agnosticism echoes similar sentiments, suggesting that if God exists, He’s merely toying with us.

Allah ﷻ categorically rejects this. Creation has purpose. And what fortifies this truth? وَإِنَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ لَـَٔاتِيَةٌ (the Hour is certainly coming). Without the Day of Judgment, one cannot truly appreciate that the world has purpose. Those who reject the Day of Judgment naturally conclude that life is pointless - just live, enjoy what you can, and move on.

Having established this reality, Allah ﷻ gives the Prophet ﷺ a profound instruction: فَٱصْفَحِ ٱلصَّفْحَ ٱلْجَمِيلَ (turn the page in the most beautiful way). The word صَفْح means to turn a page, and it’s connected to forgiveness because forgiving is like turning a page - you no longer see what was on the previous page.

The disbelievers’ rejection isn’t because the revelation lacks conviction. They’ve adopted an attitude that life itself has no purpose, so they have no interest in hearing about an afterlife. When a person becomes so engrossed in worldly things that revelation has penetrated deep into their resistance, no amount of revelation or argument suffices. The discussion just goes in circles. So Allah ﷻ tells His Messenger ﷺ: let it go, turn the page, don’t be overwhelmed by their rejection.

Ayah 86

إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ ٱلْخَلَّـٰقُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ

After telling the Prophet ﷺ to turn the page, Allah ﷻ reminds him: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ ٱلْخَلَّـٰقُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (Your Master, He truly is the Repeated Creator, and He is fully knowledgeable). The word الْخَلَّـٰقُ uses an intensive form, indicating continuous, repeated creation. Allah ﷻ keeps creating, and He knows completely what He creates and why He creates it.

Ayah 87

وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَـٰكَ سَبْعًا مِّنَ ٱلْمَثَانِى وَٱلْقُرْءَانَ ٱلْعَظِيمَ

This remarkable ayah comes as a response to the disbelievers who mocked the Prophet ﷺ saying: “What have you been given? You talk about this God who owns the skies and the earth - look at you, you’re nothing. You were living off your wife’s finances. And you’re going to talk to us about the One who holds all treasures?”

Allah ﷻ responds: وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَـٰكَ سَبْعًا مِّنَ ٱلْمَثَانِى وَٱلْقُرْءَانَ ٱلْعَظِيمَ (We have given you seven from the highly praised ones and the Great Quran).

The majority of scholars agree that سَبْعًا مِّنَ ٱلْمَثَانِى refers to Surah al-Fatihah, the seven verses that open the Quran. While there are approximately 20-30 different scholarly opinions on what this phrase might mean, the predominant view identifies it as al-Fatihah. The term is feminine (المثاني) which grammatically aligns with referring to ayat (verses).

Notice how Allah ﷻ mentions al-Fatihah separately from وَٱلْقُرْءَانَ ٱلْعَظِيمَ (the Great Quran), giving it a unique status. This is reflected in the very organization of the Quran itself. Al-Fatihah stands by itself, then you have the next three surahs, then five, then seven, then nine, eleven, thirteen - a pattern of 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13. Al-Fatihah is like the prelude or introduction before the main text begins.

The message is clear: You’ve been given something greater than any worldly treasure. You’ve been given al-Fatihah and the Great Quran.

Ayah 88

لَا تَمُدَّنَّ عَيْنَيْكَ إِلَىٰ مَا مَتَّعْنَا بِهِۦٓ أَزْوَٰجًا مِّنْهُمْ وَلَا تَحْزَنْ عَلَيْهِمْ وَٱخْفِضْ جَنَاحَكَ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

Having established what the Prophet ﷺ has been given, Allah ﷻ now gives him crucial guidance: لَا تَمُدَّنَّ عَيْنَيْكَ إِلَىٰ مَا مَتَّعْنَا بِهِۦٓ أَزْوَٰجًا مِّنْهُمْ (Don’t let your eyes stretch toward what We have provided as enjoyment to groups among them).

The word أَزْوَٰجًا is significant. We’ve encountered various Quranic words for groups - شِيَع ,فِرَق, أَحْزَاب - but azwaj is unique. The word زَوْج normally means one of a pair, part of a complementary pair. When used for a group, it indicates people who are completely cohesive, unanimous, strongly united in what they have in common. They’re completely on the same page - that’s what makes them a zawj.

Allah ﷻ is saying: those groups that are united in their disbelief, who I’ve given wealth and power - don’t let your eyes stretch toward what they have. وَلَا تَحْزَنْ عَلَيْهِمْ (and don’t grieve over them). Don’t be saddened by their state or what they’ve been given.

Then comes the crucial instruction: وَٱخْفِضْ جَنَاحَكَ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (and lower your wing for the believers). The phrase خَفْضُ الْجَنَاح literally describes a bird lowering its wings to protect its nest. This expression of complete humility and care will appear again in Surah al-Isra’ regarding parents, but here it’s used for the Prophet’s attitude toward the believers.

This guidance carries deep philosophical implications that can be difficult to accept, especially in our current political circumstances. Muslims today live in challenging situations, and we often seek alliances wherever we can find them. If legislation threatens to ban hijab in public spaces, we naturally want to ally with humanitarian groups and civil rights organizations. But many of these groups have allegiances that are questionable from an Islamic standpoint.

The political temptation is to say: “We shouldn’t speak against them because it might backfire. We’re a minority, they’re a minority - we should all just play the game together.” While this might sound reasonable as political strategy, the Prophet ﷺ was never concerned with such calculations.

Consider his situation in Makkah: he was being beaten, tortured, humiliated. If a few influential Makkans became Muslim, it would be huge for the cause. It would help keep Muslims safe. If people saw someone like Abu Sufyan walking with the Muslims, they would back off. This would be an effective strategy - targeting the elite, because even if one elite person accepts Islam, it has massive impact.

This mentality persists in Muslim communities today. We share videos about famous people who are Muslim - athletes, actors, celebrities - thinking somehow their conversion validates Islam to the broader society or changes public perception.

But Allah ﷻ tells His Messenger ﷺ: never pursue this at the expense of those who are politically, socially, and economically insignificant. Who believed with the Prophet ﷺ in Makkah? Slaves who had no status. Youth. Foreigners like Salman al-Farsi (RA) and Suhayb ar-Rumi (RA) who had no clout. Some of the poorest members of society. One or two wealthy individuals, but even they weren’t particularly powerful. Eventually Umar (RA) and others joined, but for a long time it was a very weak group politically and militarily.

Yet Allah ﷻ tells the Messenger ﷺ: I’ve given you something precious. Stay humble to these believers. Your attention should be on them. Be grateful to them. This instruction reverses worldly logic entirely.

Ayah 89

وَقُلْ إِنِّىٓ أَنَا ٱلنَّذِيرُ ٱلْمُبِينُ

While you show humility to the believers, when it comes to the elite who reject the message: وَقُلْ إِنِّىٓ أَنَا ٱلنَّذِيرُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (tell them: I am the one who openly warns).

The instruction reverses their intimidation. They thought they were warning him, threatening him. Allah ﷻ tells him to respond: No, I’m the one issuing the warnings, not you. Your warnings are empty. My warnings are real.

Ayah 90-91

كَمَآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَى ٱلْمُقْتَسِمِينَ - ٱلَّذِينَ جَعَلُوا۟ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ عِضِينَ

These ayahs present some interpretive discussion. كَمَآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَى ٱلْمُقْتَسِمِينَ (Just as We sent down upon those who divided things up) - scholars differ on who المقتسمين refers to. Some believe it refers to the Jews who divided up the Book. Others believe it describes a tactic used by some Qurayshi leaders against the Quran.

These leaders would say to each other: “You take this surah, I’ll take that one” - and they would make fun of different parts of the Quran, dividing it up among themselves to mock it systematically. The next ayah clarifies: ٱلَّذِينَ جَعَلُوا۟ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ عِضِينَ (those who took the Quran and tore it to pieces, ripped it apart).

But this idea of ripping the Quran apart isn’t limited to that historical practice. It applies to anyone who approaches the Quran in a fragmented way - accepting some parts while ignoring others. When someone says “I like this part of the Quran, but the rest doesn’t exist for me,” they’ve literally ripped the Quran apart. When someone knows only one or two ayahs and considers that sufficient, they’ve done the same.

Some scholars take this principle even further, arguing that this is why we should study the Quran cohesively as an integrated text, not piecemeal. There are different approaches to Quranic study - you can study individual ayahs through tafsir, going from one ayah to the next. But there’s also the approach of understanding how the surah comes together as a whole: how the ayahs connect with each other, how the beginning and ending relate, how surahs connect with each other, how groups of surahs formulate arguments together.

This comprehensive approach prevents us from making judgments about revelation based on isolated sections. We need a holistic view of the Book of Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 92-93

فَوَرَبِّكَ لَنَسْـَٔلَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ - عَمَّا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ

Now comes a powerful oath: فَوَرَبِّكَ لَنَسْـَٔلَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (I swear by your Master, We will absolutely interrogate them - all of them, all together). عَمَّا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (about what they used to do).

This connects directly back to the beginning of the surah. Remember how it opened? ذَرْهُمْ يَأْكُلُوا۟ وَيَتَمَتَّعُوا۟ وَيُلْهِهِمُ ٱلْأَمَلُ (Leave them alone - let them eat, let them enjoy themselves, let them be distracted by their false hopes).

The message was: You leave them alone, but I won’t. The beginning said “leave them,” and now the ending says “We will interrogate them.” You don’t have to confront them about everything - We will question them about what they used to do.

Ayah 94

فَٱصْدَعْ بِمَا تُؤْمَرُ وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ

The instruction continues: فَٱصْدَعْ بِمَا تُؤْمَرُ (Remain at what you’ve been commanded). The word اصْدَعْ comes from صَدْع which means to accomplish something, to get something done despite difficulty. It carries the sense of persistence through hardship.

Allah ﷻ is saying: I know it’s hard, but stay at it. Keep doing what you’re doing. وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (and turn away from those who commit shirk). This echoes the opening instruction: ذَرْهُمْ (leave them alone). Now it’s أَعْرِضْ (turn away from them, ignore them).

Ayah 95-96

إِنَّا كَفَيْنَـٰكَ ٱلْمُسْتَهْزِءِينَ - ٱلَّذِينَ يَجْعَلُونَ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ ۚ فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ

Allah ﷻ makes a powerful promise: إِنَّا كَفَيْنَـٰكَ ٱلْمُسْتَهْزِءِينَ (We are enough for you against those who make fun of you).

When did they mock him? At the very beginning of the surah: إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا ٱلذِّكْرَ وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ لَحَـٰفِظُونَ - وَلَقَدِ ٱسْتُهْزِئَ بِرُسُلٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ (We have sent down the Reminder and We are its protectors - and messengers before you were certainly mocked). Now Allah ﷻ says: I am enough for you against those mockers.

ٱلَّذِينَ يَجْعَلُونَ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ (those who take gods to worship and obey besides Allah) - فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ (soon they will find out).

When will they find out? This was already mentioned at the beginning of the surah: رُّبَمَا يَوَدُّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَوْ كَانُوا۟ مُسْلِمِينَ (Soon those who disbelieve will wish they had been Muslims).

Ayah 97

وَلَقَدْ نَعْلَمُ أَنَّكَ يَضِيقُ صَدْرُكَ بِمَا يَقُولُونَ

Allah ﷻ acknowledges the Prophet’s human response: وَلَقَدْ نَعْلَمُ أَنَّكَ يَضِيقُ صَدْرُكَ بِمَا يَقُولُونَ (We already know that your chest becomes tight because of the things they say).

What did they say? Go back to the beginning of the surah: وَقَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِى نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ ٱلذِّكْرُ إِنَّكَ لَمَجْنُونٌ - لَّوْ مَا تَأْتِينَا بِٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (They said: O you upon whom the Reminder has been sent down, you are insane. Why don’t you bring us angels if you are truthful?)

These accusations naturally caused tightness in his chest. But notice what Allah ﷻ doesn’t say. He doesn’t give the Prophet ﷺ strategies for dealing with his enemies or refuting their arguments in detail.

Ayah 98

فَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ وَكُن مِّنَ ٱلسَّـٰجِدِينَ

Instead, Allah ﷻ gives him the remedy: فَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ (declare the perfection of your Master by praising Him) وَكُن مِّنَ ٱلسَّـٰجِدِينَ (and be among those who make sujud).

This teaches us something profound about dealing with difficulty. When your enemies are many and they’re saying horrific things, the Prophet ﷺ is told to continue preaching the word of Allah ﷻ. And to deal with it personally, internally - what should he do? Make tasbih and make sujud.

Allah ﷻ isn’t teaching him how to deal with his enemies. He’s teaching him how to find peace despite his enemies. Your chest became tight? Let Me tell you what will alleviate that tightness and bring tranquility to your heart: فَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ وَكُن مِّنَ ٱلسَّـٰجِدِينَ (make tasbih and be in a state of sujud).

Focus your attention not on your problems but on the greatness of Allah ﷻ. When you’re doing sujud and making tasbih, what are you thinking about? Not your enemies - Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 99

وَٱعْبُدْ رَبَّكَ حَتَّىٰ يَأْتِيَكَ ٱلْيَقِينُ

The surah concludes with a comprehensive command: وَٱعْبُدْ رَبَّكَ حَتَّىٰ يَأْتِيَكَ ٱلْيَقِينُ (Continue to enslave yourself to your Master until the certainty comes to you).

اَلْيَقِينُ - the certainty, the absolute reality - is most commonly interpreted as death. This is the one thing nobody has doubt about; everyone will experience it.

The instruction means: until death arrives, you will be in this state of worship. Of course, you need to worship Allah ﷻ throughout your life until you die. But there’s a special emphasis here: especially when you’re going through difficult times, even you, O Messenger ﷺ, must go out of your way to engage in ibadah.

This is the prescription for dealing with opposition, mockery, and the tightness in the chest that comes from being rejected and opposed. The remedy isn’t sophisticated debate strategies or political maneuvering. The remedy is turning to Allah ﷻ in worship - through tasbih, through sujud, through continuous ibadah until death.

This conclusion beautifully ties the entire surah together, bringing us back to where we started while providing the spiritual tools needed to persevere in the face of rejection and hostility.
